title: for Loops
subtitle: Stop copy-pasting! Teach Python to repeat things for you.
goal: Use for loops to repeat actions, walk through lists and strings, count with range(), and build up running totals.

blocks.heading: Why loops? 🔁
blocks.html:
Imagine you want to say “Hello!” five times. You could write five print lines... but that's a lot of typing, and what if you wanted it 100 times? 😵

A loop lets you say: “Do this thing, over and over.” You write the action once, and Python repeats it for you. Lazy in the best way!

blocks.heading: Looping over a list
blocks.html:
Remember lists from Day 8? A for loop can walk through a list one item at a time. The pattern looks like this:

for friend in friends:

This means: “For each item in friends, call it friend, and run the indented lines below.” The indented part is the body of the loop — Python runs it once for every item.

blocks.caption: Greet every friend in a list
blocks.code:
friends = ["Mia", "Leo", "Zoe"]
for friend in friends:
    print("Hi " + friend + "!")
print("That's everyone!")
blocks.explain: The loop runs once for each name. 'friend' holds Mia, then Leo, then Zoe. The last print() is NOT indented, so it runs only once at the end.

blocks.html: Indentation is everything! The lines inside a loop must be pushed in with 4 spaces. That spacing is how Python knows which lines belong to the loop. Line it up neatly and it just works.

blocks.heading: Looping over a string's letters
blocks.html: A string is just a row of letters, so a for loop can walk through it too — one character at a time.

blocks.caption: Print each letter of a word
blocks.explain: Each time around the loop, 'letter' holds the next character: P, then Y, then T, and so on until the word ends.

blocks.heading: Counting with range() 🔢
blocks.html:
What if you don't have a list — you just want to count? That's what range() is for.

  • range(5) → 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 (starts at 0, stops before 5)
  • range(1, 6) → 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (start at 1, stop before 6)
  • range(0, 10, 2) → 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 (count by 2s — the last number is the step)

The stop number is never included — Python always stops just before it. Sneaky but useful!

blocks.caption: Counting and a countdown
blocks.explain: range(1, 6) counts 1 to 5. range(5, 0, -1) uses a step of -1 to count DOWN: 5, 4, 3, 2, 1. A negative step makes the loop go backwards.

blocks.question: What numbers does range(1, 4) give you?
blocks.explain: range(start, stop) begins at 1 and stops just BEFORE 4 — so you get 1, 2, 3.

blocks.heading: The accumulator pattern ➕
blocks.html:
Here's a super-useful trick. Make a variable before the loop, then add to it each time around. This is called accumulating — like filling a piggy bank coin by coin. 🐷

You can build up a number (a running total) or even build up a string letter by letter.

blocks.caption: Add up numbers with a running total
blocks.code:
numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40]
total = 0
for n in numbers:
    total = total + n
    print("So far:", total)
print("Final total:", total)
blocks.explain: 'total' starts at 0. Each loop adds the next number to it. total = total + n means 'take the old total and make it bigger by n'. By the end it holds 100.

blocks.html: Always set up your accumulator first! Start a counter or total at 0 before the loop. For building a string, start it empty: word = "". Forgetting this is the #1 loop mistake.

blocks.caption: A multiplication table with enumerate()
blocks.code:
print("The 3 times table:")
for n in range(1, 6):
    print("3 x", n, "=", 3 * n)

print("---")
prizes = ["gold", "silver", "bronze"]
for place, prize in enumerate(prizes):
    print(place, "->", prize)
blocks.explain: range() makes a neat times table. enumerate() is a helper that hands you BOTH the position number AND the item — here place is 0, 1, 2 while prize is each medal. Handy when you want to number things!

blocks.question: Why do we write 'total = 0' BEFORE the loop instead of inside it?
options: So it starts fresh once, then the loop adds to it each time
It makes the loop run faster
blocks.explain: If you reset total to 0 inside the loop, it would wipe out your running total every time! Set it up once before, then add inside.

blocks.question: What does enumerate() give you on each loop?
options: Both a position number AND the item
blocks.explain: enumerate() hands you two things at once: the index (0, 1, 2, ...) and the item itself.

blocks.title: Count to 10
blocks.instructions: Use a for loop with range() to print the numbers 1 through 10, each on its own line. Fill in the range so it counts 1 to 10.
blocks.passMsg: Counting like a pro! Notice range(1, 11) stops AT 10. 🎉
blocks.failMsg: Remember: range stops just before the second number, so use range(1, 11) to reach 10.

blocks.title: Sum the numbers 1 to 5
blocks.instructions: Use the accumulator pattern to add up 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5. Start total at 0, loop with range(1, 6), and add each number. Print the final total.
blocks.starter:
total = 0
for n in range(1, 6):
    total = ___
print("Total:", total)
blocks.solution:
total = 0
for n in range(1, 6):
    total = total + n
print("Total:", total)
blocks.passMsg: 15! You filled the piggy bank one number at a time. 🐷
blocks.failMsg: Inside the loop, write total = total + n so each number gets added on. The answer should be 15.

challenge.title: Build a name banner
challenge.instructions: Take a word and print each letter on its own line, with an arrow. Use a for loop to walk through the letters of the word. Try it with your own name!
challenge.starter:
name = "SAM"
# loop over each letter and print it with an arrow, like:  -> S
challenge.hint: Write 'for letter in name:' then indent a print line that shows the arrow and the letter.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Banner complete! You finished Day 9 — loops are now your superpower. No more copy-paste!
